title: Dark Personality Pattern Check

introduction.subtitle: Reflective pattern check. 10-12 min.
introduction.description:
Dark Personality Pattern Check is a non-clinical reflection tool for protective behaviour against manipulation, entitlement, callousness, and impulse-boundary risk. Higher scores mean stronger protective habits, not stronger dark traits.

Answer from recent behaviour under pressure, not ideal self-description. Your answers stay in this browser unless you explicitly share a result.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Reflective self-knowledge tool only. Not a clinical diagnosis, employment assessment, forensic assessment, medical advice, or validated psychometric instrument.

questions.text: How do you persuade someone when full information might weaken your case?

options.text: A. I would leave out the information if it helps me win.
B. I would share only what I expect they can discover anyway.
C. I would disclose the material downside, but still argue my case strongly.
D. I would make my interest, their risk, and the missing information explicit before asking for agreement.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Transparent Influence: How do you persuade someone when full information might weaken your case
conceptHelp.summary: Transparent Influence reflects how clearly you protect consent and choice when persuasion could become manipulation. This question looks at: "How do you persuade someone when full information might weaken your case?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can turn influence into hidden pressure, selective disclosure, or exploitative framing.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer makes interests, risks, and missing information visible before asking for agreement.

questions.text: How do you handle a negotiation where the other side trusts you more than they should?

options.text: A. I would use the trust advantage to improve my outcome.
B. I would avoid lying, but still let their mistaken confidence work for me.
C. I would slow the negotiation enough to correct material misunderstandings.
D. I would protect their ability to choose freely even if it weakens my position.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Transparent Influence: How do you handle a negotiation where the other side trusts you more than they should
conceptHelp.summary: Transparent Influence reflects how clearly you protect consent and choice when persuasion could become manipulation. This question looks at: "How do you handle a negotiation where the other side trusts you more than they should?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can turn influence into hidden pressure, selective disclosure, or exploitative framing.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer makes interests, risks, and missing information visible before asking for agreement.

questions.text: How do you react when a shortcut would let you win without being noticed?

options.text: A. I would take the shortcut if the chance of being caught is low.
B. I might take it if the harm seems abstract or reversible.
C. I would pause and check whether the shortcut violates a real boundary.
D. I would reject the shortcut and make the boundary visible enough to prevent repeats.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Transparent Influence: How do you react when a shortcut would let you win without being noticed
conceptHelp.summary: Transparent Influence reflects how clearly you protect consent and choice when persuasion could become manipulation. This question looks at: "How do you react when a shortcut would let you win without being noticed?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can turn influence into hidden pressure, selective disclosure, or exploitative framing.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer makes interests, risks, and missing information visible before asking for agreement.

questions.text: How do you behave when someone else receives credit for shared work?

options.text: A. I feel deprived and look for ways to reclaim the spotlight.
B. I stay civil, but privately discount their contribution.
C. I recognise the shared work and add context if something important is missing.
D. I actively reinforce fair credit and use the moment to strengthen trust.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Status Humility: How do you behave when someone else receives credit for shared work
conceptHelp.summary: Status Humility reflects how well you stay fair when attention, credit, challenge, or exclusion affects your ego. This question looks at: "How do you behave when someone else receives credit for shared work?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can produce entitlement, retaliation, credit capture, or contempt for lower-status people.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer separates contribution from status and makes challenge safer.

questions.text: How do you respond when a lower-status person challenges you correctly?

options.text: A. I attack their credibility or remind them of their place.
B. I listen, but mostly to find a weakness in their argument.
C. I check the claim on its merits and adjust if they are right.
D. I thank them, correct the issue, and make it safer for others to challenge me.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Status Humility: How do you respond when a lower-status person challenges you correctly
conceptHelp.summary: Status Humility reflects how well you stay fair when attention, credit, challenge, or exclusion affects your ego. This question looks at: "How do you respond when a lower-status person challenges you correctly?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can produce entitlement, retaliation, credit capture, or contempt for lower-status people.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer separates contribution from status and makes challenge safer.

questions.text: How do you handle not being invited into an important room?

options.text: A. I assume I was excluded unfairly and look for leverage.
B. I feel slighted, but try not to show it.
C. I ask whether my involvement is actually needed and respect the answer.
D. I separate status from contribution and support the decision if it serves the work.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Status Humility: How do you handle not being invited into an important room
conceptHelp.summary: Status Humility reflects how well you stay fair when attention, credit, challenge, or exclusion affects your ego. This question looks at: "How do you handle not being invited into an important room?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can produce entitlement, retaliation, credit capture, or contempt for lower-status people.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer separates contribution from status and makes challenge safer.

questions.text: How do you treat people who block an outcome you care about?

options.text: A. I treat them as an obstacle to move or pressure.
B. I stay professional, but stop caring about their constraints.
C. I try to understand the constraint while still pursuing the outcome.
D. I protect dignity, boundaries, and facts even when I strongly disagree.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Empathy Under Pressure: How do you treat people who block an outcome you care about
conceptHelp.summary: Empathy Under Pressure reflects how well you preserve dignity and harm awareness when stakes are high. This question looks at: "How do you treat people who block an outcome you care about?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can make people with less power carry avoidable cost or fear.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer names who may be harmed and protects boundaries while still making decisions.

questions.text: How do you make trade-offs that impose costs on people with less power?

options.text: A. I focus on the goal and let weaker parties absorb the cost.
B. I notice the cost, but accept it if no one objects strongly.
C. I identify who bears the cost and look for mitigation before deciding.
D. I make the harm visible, include affected voices where possible, and set a boundary on acceptable trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Empathy Under Pressure: How do you make trade-offs that impose costs on people with less power
conceptHelp.summary: Empathy Under Pressure reflects how well you preserve dignity and harm awareness when stakes are high. This question looks at: "How do you make trade-offs that impose costs on people with less power?".

conceptHelp.example: A strong answer names who may be harmed and protects boundaries while still making decisions.

questions.text: How do you react when someone shows vulnerability you could use against them?

options.text: A. I would remember the vulnerability for future leverage.
B. I would not use it immediately, but might if conflict escalates.
C. I would treat it as private unless there is a serious safety reason.
D. I would protect the vulnerability and avoid creating dependence or fear.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Empathy Under Pressure: How do you react when someone shows vulnerability you could use against them
conceptHelp.summary: Empathy Under Pressure reflects how well you preserve dignity and harm awareness when stakes are high. This question looks at: "How do you react when someone shows vulnerability you could use against them?".

questions.text: How do you behave when you are angry and have access to damaging information?

options.text: A. I might use the information if it teaches them a lesson.
B. I would probably hold back publicly, but might threaten privately.
C. I would pause until I can decide without retaliation driving the choice.
D. I would protect the information, step away, and use a fair process if action is needed.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Impulse & Boundary Control: How do you behave when you are angry and have access to damaging information
conceptHelp.summary: Impulse & Boundary Control reflects how well you protect agreed limits when anger, reward, or convenience pulls the other way. This question looks at: "How do you behave when you are angry and have access to damaging information?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can turn temporary emotion or reward into lasting trust damage.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer uses pauses, checks, and fair process before acting.

questions.text: How do you handle rules that feel inconvenient but protect other people?

options.text: A. I ignore the rule when it blocks my preferred outcome.
B. I comply when watched, but look for exceptions when inconvenient.
C. I follow the rule unless there is a transparent, agreed reason to change it.
D. I treat protective rules as trust infrastructure and raise problems through a fair process.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Impulse & Boundary Control: How do you handle rules that feel inconvenient but protect other people
conceptHelp.summary: Impulse & Boundary Control reflects how well you protect agreed limits when anger, reward, or convenience pulls the other way. This question looks at: "How do you handle rules that feel inconvenient but protect other people?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Weak protection here can turn temporary emotion or reward into lasting trust damage.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.

questions.text: How do you respond when immediate reward conflicts with a boundary you agreed to?

options.text: A. I take the reward and rationalise the boundary later.
B. I hesitate, but may bend the boundary if the reward is large.
C. I keep the boundary and look for an acceptable alternative.
D. I pre-commit checks that make it hard for immediate reward to override the boundary.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Impulse & Boundary Control: How do you respond when immediate reward conflicts with a boundary you agreed to

questions.text: How do you handle information that would make your proposal look less attractive?

options.text: A. I would omit it if disclosure might cost me the win.
B. I would mention it only if directly asked.
C. I would disclose the material issue and explain why I still think the proposal is valid.
D. I would make the downside easy to understand and invite the other person to decide with full context.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do you react when someone sets a boundary that reduces your influence?

options.text: A. I look for another route around the boundary.
B. I accept it outwardly, but keep testing where it can bend.
C. I respect it and ask what communication is still welcome.
D. I treat the boundary as useful information and adjust without pressure or punishment.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do you use private knowledge about someone’s insecurity?

options.text: A. I may use it if it helps move them toward the outcome.
B. I avoid obvious cruelty, but still frame things around that insecurity.
C. I keep it out of persuasion unless it is directly relevant and safe to discuss.
D. I protect the information and choose an influence path that does not exploit vulnerability.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do you behave when someone junior outperforms you publicly?

options.text: A. I minimise their achievement or look for a flaw.
B. I congratulate them, but privately feel the need to reassert position.
C. I acknowledge the result and learn what they did well.
D. I amplify their contribution and use the moment to strengthen the team standard.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.summary: Status Humility reflects how well you stay fair when attention, credit, challenge, or exclusion affects your ego. This question looks at: "How do you behave when someone junior outperforms you publicly?".

questions.text: How do you respond when a decision benefits the group but reduces your status?

options.text: A. I resist it or reframe the decision so I keep status.
B. I accept it only if my loss of status is not visible.
C. I support it if the evidence shows it is better for the group.
D. I support it openly and help make the transition clean for others.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Status Humility: How do you respond when a decision benefits the group but reduces your status

questions.text: How do you handle feedback that you came across as arrogant or dismissive?

options.text: A. I dismiss the feedback as oversensitivity.
B. I defend my intent before considering the impact.
C. I ask for examples and adjust the behaviour if the pattern is real.
D. I thank the person, repair the specific impact, and invite earlier signals next time.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Status Humility: How do you handle feedback that you came across as arrogant or dismissive

questions.text: How do you respond when achieving the goal requires someone else to carry a burden?

options.text: A. I focus on the goal and let them absorb the burden.
B. I notice the burden, but treat it as the cost of progress.
C. I check whether the burden is necessary and look for mitigation.
D. I make the burden visible, negotiate it fairly, and protect non-negotiable limits.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do you talk about people who are no longer useful to your plan?

options.text: A. I talk about them as obstacles or dead weight.
B. I stay polite publicly, but dismiss them privately.
C. I separate their current fit from their dignity and past contribution.
D. I keep language fair, protect confidentiality, and avoid turning usefulness into human worth.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How do you behave when a mistake by someone else threatens your outcome?

options.text: A. I make sure they feel the consequence so it does not happen again.
B. I control my reaction, but focus mainly on assigning fault.
C. I stabilise the outcome, then review responsibility and prevention.
D. I protect the person from humiliation while still making accountability and repair explicit.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.summary: Empathy Under Pressure reflects how well you preserve dignity and harm awareness when stakes are high. This question looks at: "How do you behave when a mistake by someone else threatens your outcome?".

questions.text: How do you handle a tempting opportunity that depends on bending an agreement?

options.text: A. I take it if the agreement seems unlikely to be enforced.
B. I look for a technical interpretation that lets me proceed.
C. I pause and ask whether the agreement would still feel fair if disclosed.
D. I either renegotiate transparently or decline the opportunity.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Impulse & Boundary Control: How do you handle a tempting opportunity that depends on bending an agreement
conceptHelp.summary: Impulse & Boundary Control reflects how well you protect agreed limits when anger, reward, or convenience pulls the other way. This question looks at: "How do you handle a tempting opportunity that depends on bending an agreement?".

conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer from recent behaviour under stress, power, threat, or temptation. Do not score the person you wish you were.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer uses pauses, checks, and fair process before acting.

questions.text: How do you behave when retaliation would feel justified?

options.text: A. I retaliate if it restores balance or teaches a lesson.
B. I do not retaliate openly, but may create consequences indirectly.
C. I delay action until I can separate repair, boundary-setting, and punishment.
D. I use a fair process and avoid actions whose main purpose is to hurt back.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Impulse & Boundary Control: How do you behave when retaliation would feel justified

questions.text: How do you respond when secrecy would make a questionable action easier?

options.text: A. I keep it quiet if the outcome is useful.
B. I tell only people who are likely to agree with me.
C. I ask what would change if the action had to be explained to affected people.
D. I bring the decision into an appropriate review before acting.
Not sure / not applicable.

questionStageDescription: Prefer recent observable behaviour over ideal self-image. If unsure, choose the answer a trusted observer would recognise.
